confidence_rationale: Core MVP feasibility is strong if imports are file/manual first and AI, real-time sync, portals, and deep integrations are deferred. Supplier credentials and broad connector automation remain architecture-sensitive risks.

core_mvp_feasibility.summary: Supplier records, product/service import, unified asset library, pricing logic, bundles/recipes, and document output are technically feasible with common web application architecture.
limitations: No supplier API inventory has been validated.
No data volume assumptions have been tested.

supplier_integration_feasibility.summary: Broad supplier portal synchronization is feasible only as a staged program and should not be an MVP dependency.
limitations: Supplier-specific APIs, exports, credential policies, and terms are unverified.

ai_feasibility.summary: AI assistance for categorization, summarization, extraction, search, pricing suggestions, and quote drafting is plausible after structured data and review workflows exist.
limitations: AI should not be launch-critical until data quality, human review, hallucination controls, and cost controls are defined.

integration_feasibility.mvp_recommendation: Start with file/manual import and import templates.
stage_3_constraints: Separate supplier source model from normalized asset model.
Design connector interfaces as replaceable adapters.
Do not hardwire any single supplier portal into the domain model.
Treat supplier credentials as high-sensitivity secrets.

support_burden.summary: Messy supplier files and service pricing tables will create onboarding and support load.
support_burden.mitigation: Supported import templates, import preview, clear validation errors, and vertical-specific onboarding.

approval_workflows.summary: Pricing and quote approvals are useful but can slow users if overbuilt.
approval_workflows.mitigation: Start with simple draft, review, approved, sent states.

infrastructure_constraints: Use asynchronous import jobs for larger supplier files.
Store files, images, and documents separately from normalized asset records.
Track import provenance and field mapping decisions.
Design pricing calculations for repeatability and auditability.
Separate AI suggestions from approved source-of-truth fields.

architecture_warnings.warning: Supplier credentials and portal automation require secrets management, least privilege, auditing, lifecycle controls, and terms review before implementation.
The unified asset model must represent products, services, bundles, recipes, documents, pricing rules, and customer-facing outputs without collapsing into a generic blob schema.
Document generation should be isolated from pricing logic so quotes, price books, catalogs, and proposals can evolve independently.

evidence.summary: OWASP guidance supports centralized and standardized secrets management, access controls, lifecycle management, auditing, and rotation.
evidence.confidence_effect: raises technical feasibility only if credential automation is architected correctly
evidence.limitations: General security guidance, not supplier-specific API validation.

evidence.summary: Adjacent competitors demonstrate feasibility of quotes, invoices, catalogs, purchase orders, customer management, reporting, and automation in print-shop software.
evidence.limitations: Does not prove Source-Blendr's broader supplier aggregation architecture.

unresolved_unknowns: Expected import volume, media/document volume, and sync frequency.
Whether credentials must be stored for MVP or can be avoided through file import.
recommendations: Stage 3 should design connector boundaries but implement file/manual import first.
Stage 3 should treat AI as a non-critical service boundary with human approval.
Stage 3 should model pricing, costs, bundles, recipes, and generated outputs as separate but traceable domains.
